caveats: a: No labor-compensation ratio is available for this industry.
a: Public occupation evidence is not citrus-specific.
rho: Bounded judgment rather than an observed adoption rate.
rho: AI cannot remove biological and weather variability.
e: The supplied firm count and labor share are missing and must not be inferred.
e: Farm, establishment, parcel, and operating company are not equivalent.
s5: Economy-wide demographic proxy.
s5: No denominator-based transfer study for 111320.
q: No public contract sample quantifies pass-through.
q: Fresh and processed fruit have different bargaining structures.
d5: The code mixes fruits and regions with very different trajectories.
d5: One-season forecasts are weak five-year predictors.
o: Demand may shift among regions, imports, and operator types.
o: This is separate from labor automation within groves.

lens.customer_and_revenue_model: Seasonal but recurring crop sales under spot, marketing-cooperative, processor, packinghouse, retail, or supply-contract arrangements, supported by grove management, harvesting, compliance, and quality grading.
lens.excluded_activities: Orange groves, hobby farms, landholding shells without operations, captive groves, passive leases, and firms outside the EBITDA band.
lens.included_activities: Independent LMM growers and grove operators of grapefruit, lemons, limes, mandarins, tangerines, and other non-orange citrus that repeatedly sell fruit to external packers, processors, distributors, retailers, or contracted buyers.

a.bridge: BLS describes crop, nursery, and greenhouse farmworkers across all crops rather than citrus and states they perform growing and harvesting tasks. I map crop forecasting, scouting-image review, irrigation and spray planning, labor scheduling, compliance records, purchasing, sales administration, and grading support as exposed; pruning, spraying, irrigation repair, harvesting, equipment operation, and field inspection are less substitutable. The broad crop proxy may overstate exposure in harvest-intensive groves.
caveats: No labor-compensation ratio is available for this industry.
Public occupation evidence is not citrus-specific.
a.change_evidence: Payroll-weighted task diaries from 10-15 California, Florida, Texas, and Arizona non-orange citrus operations, including current sensor and software use, would materially revise the estimate.
a.rationale: Knowledge and administrative work can be augmented, but most grove labor is physical, seasonal, and field-based.

d5.bridge: USDA's January 2026 Florida forecast puts grapefruit at 1.20 million boxes, 8% below prior-season utilization, while its 2025/26 outlook shows different directions across California citrus categories. I translate this near-term regional and product evidence into a declining central case for the current U.S. basket, driven by Florida disease and weather, with upside from California and protected production. The proxy can overstate decline for lemon and mandarin-heavy firms outside Florida.
caveats: The code mixes fruits and regions with very different trajectories.
One-season forecasts are weak five-year predictors.
d5.change_evidence: USDA NASS five-year acreage, bearing-tree, yield, and utilized-production series by fruit and state, plus HLB prevalence, water allocations, and replanting plans, would materially revise the range.
d5.rationale: Citrus greening, storms, water constraints, urban development, and imports pressure domestic output; protected growing, replanting, and resilient western production create upside.

caveats: The supplied firm count and labor share are missing and must not be inferred.
Farm, establishment, parcel, and operating company are not equivalent.
e.change_evidence: A firm census using USDA Census of Agriculture microdata, state grove registrations, business databases, land records, packinghouse supplier lists, and normalized EBITDA would establish eligibility.
e.rationale: Commercial independent groves with recurring external fruit sales fit, but family landholdings, hobby farms, captive processor acreage, passive leases, grove-management arrangements without crop ownership, distressed Florida acreage, and businesses below or above the band reduce eligibility.

l.detail: SUSB scope excludes this activity, no IPS labor-compensation/output series at any level, and no other receipts source downloaded; labor_share left null (recorded gap).
l.rationale: dataset labor_share has no defensible value

n.detail: margin 0.0804 (margins.json[11]: Damodaran Jan 2026: Farming/Agriculture EBITDA/Sales 8.04%); revenue/employee $299,458 (SUSB 2022 receipts/employment at 11); DATA GAP: no SUSB size-class data for this code; n_band could not be computed — the research run must supply it with a shown chain, quality ESTIMATE
n.rationale: dataset n_band has no defensible value

caveats: Demand may shift among regions, imports, and operator types.
This is separate from labor automation within groves.
o.change_evidence: Evidence of material consumer substitution away from these fruits, widespread controlled-environment production by non-grove operators, or processor vertical integration would move the estimate.
o.rationale: Retained demand requires an accountable grower to maintain trees, manage water and pests, harvest, and deliver physical fruit. Software can improve decisions but cannot produce fruit without operated land and biological assets.

caveats: No public contract sample quantifies pass-through.
Fresh and processed fruit have different bargaining structures.
q.change_evidence: Marketing and processor contracts, cooperative pool rules, packout and price histories, land leases, and buyer interviews would directly test benefit retention.
q.rationale: Short-run yield and operating savings can accrue to the grower, especially under fixed supply agreements. Commodity pricing, packinghouse terms, processor bargaining, imports, cooperative pooling, and land rents transmit savings over time.

caveats: Bounded judgment rather than an observed adoption rate.
AI cannot remove biological and weather variability.
rho.change_evidence: Field trials measuring scouting hours, input use, yield, false disease alerts, schedule efficiency, and administrative hours across regions would move the range.
rho.rationale: Scouting, forecasting, irrigation planning, recordkeeping, scheduling, and commercial support can be implemented within five years, but fragmented farm data, connectivity, seasonal workers, biological uncertainty, equipment integration, and grower change capacity constrain realization.

s5.bridge: Federal Reserve owner-age evidence covers small businesses generally rather than citrus farms. I use older-owner succession pressure and discount for family transfers, land sales without transferable operations, passive lease changes, closures, and internal reorganizations. Disease and development value may increase exits but also turn them into land transactions outside the lens.
caveats: Economy-wide demographic proxy.
No denominator-based transfer study for 111320.
s5.change_evidence: USDA Census of Agriculture operator-age and succession data, county land deeds, grove business sales, packinghouse supplier attrition, and agricultural broker interviews since 2017 would move the estimate.
s5.rationale: Succession and land-value pressure create control events, but many are family transfers or grove closures rather than qualifying transfers of ongoing operations.

principal_driver: Repeat physical food demand and data-rich agronomic planning create a modest augmentation opportunity.
principal_weakness: Disease and climate exposure combine with missing firm and labor inputs to make the opportunity unusually uncertain.

caveats: S1 and S2 establish broad agricultural duties and the NAICS boundary, but they do not directly quantify the citrus-specific labor exposure, realization, eligibility, or retained-demand estimates they contextualize.

S3, S4, and S6 establish real disease pressure, protected-production potential, and divergent product and regional trends, but short-run Florida and California evidence is a limited bridge to a five-year national non-orange-citrus d5 of 0.88.
S4 does not provide contract, pass-through, or firm-population evidence for q or e; both are appropriately labeled ESTIMATE and disclose the missing evidence.
S5 does not observe succession or qualifying control transfers, so s5 remains a broad economy-wide demographic proxy despite its explicit bridge and wide range.
The independent-LMM-operator lens is coherent, but the record has no direct evidence for the prevalence of qualifying operators; this is honestly reflected in estimated e and missing n.
Because l and n are missing, the record has no base tier and remains a stress-test-only EVIDENCE_FIRST screen with a maximally broad attainable tier interval.

sources_audited.note: BLS confirms 504,800 crop, nursery, and greenhouse farmworkers in 2024 and a predominantly physical task mix including planting, pruning, irrigation, harvesting, packing, pesticide application, and equipment work. It is a broad occupation source, not citrus-specific evidence for the numeric a value or retained-demand o estimate.

The Census definition directly confirms that 111320 covers citrus groves except oranges and lists grapefruit, lemon, mandarin, tangelo, and tangerine groves, with orange groves classified separately. It supports the industry boundary but does not quantify a, rho, e, or o.

USDA NASS directly reports the January 2026 Florida grapefruit forecast of 1.20 million boxes, 8 percent below the prior season's 1.30 million-box utilization. This is one product, state, and season, so it only partially supports the five-year national-basket d5 proxy and does not directly measure rho or o.

USDA ERS supports product and regional heterogeneity: 2025/26 California tangerines and lemons are down while grapefruit is up, and the U.S. grapefruit forecast is above the prior season but below its preceding five-year average. It does not quantify eligibility e or benefit retention q, and one-season forecasts are limited support for five-year d5.

The Federal Reserve chartbook is a 2023 survey of small employer firms organized by primary-owner age, so it supplies broad owner-age context. It does not measure five-year succession or qualifying control transfers, and it is neither citrus-specific nor a denominator-based estimate for s5.

University of Florida directly states that protective screens exclude the Asian citrus psyllid, the vector for citrus greening, and reports Florida grapefruit production falling from 7.8 to 4.1 million boxes from 2016/17 to 2020/21, described as a 53 percent decline.

sources.supports: BLS describes crop farmworkers as performing numerous growing and harvesting tasks and reports 504,800 crop, nursery, and greenhouse farmworkers.
sources.title: Agricultural Workers

sources.supports: Census identifies citrus groves other than oranges as 111320 and classifies orange growing separately.
sources.title: NAICS Definition File: Citrus Groves

sources.supports: USDA forecasts Florida grapefruit at 1.20 million boxes, 8% below the prior season's 1.30 million-box utilization.
sources.title: Florida Citrus January 2026 Forecast

sources.supports: USDA reports divergent 2025/26 California citrus forecasts, with tangerines and lemons down and grapefruit up, supporting regional and product heterogeneity.
sources.title: USDA Fruit and Tree Nuts Outlook: March 2026

sources.supports: Federal Reserve owner-age evidence used only as a broad succession proxy.
sources.title: 2024 Chartbook on Firms by Age of Primary Owner

sources.supports: University of Florida explains that protective screens exclude the pest vector for citrus greening and reports Florida grapefruit production falling 53% from 2016/17 to 2020/21.
sources.title: Florida Citrus Production Guide: Citrus Under Protective Screen

title: Citrus (except Orange) Groves
